one_line_takeaway: Single-turn refusal benchmarks systematically mis-rank LLM safety-helpfulness calibration: CARRYONBENCH shows models that look safest under one-shot seemingly-harmful queries often converge to similar conversation-level harmfulness once benign users clarify intent across turns, while their capacity to actually recover useful information diverges sharply and predictably by three distinct failure modes (lock-in, unsafe recovery, repetition).
key_finding: At Turn 1 (query alone), models fulfill only 10.5-37.6% of the benign information checklist; with the same query paired with the full benign intent upfront (Oracle single-turn), fulfillment rises to 25.1-72.1%, showing the Turn-1 shortfall is driven by intent misinterpretation, not missing knowledge. Under multi-turn conversations with at least one benign clarification (BC), 13 of 14 models approach or exceed their own Oracle single-turn utility (BC range 37.0-75.2%), meaning accumulated conversational context often outperforms stating intent upfront in one shot. Under no-clarification (NC) multi-turn conversations (pushback/rephrasing/non-contentful moves only), utility gains are modest (+4.6 to +19.9 percentage points) versus Turn 1, showing genuine intent-revealing clarification -- not just extra turns -- drives recovery. The paper identifies and quantifies three failure modes invisible to single-turn evaluation: (1) utility lock-in -- e.g., DeepSeek-V3.1 is a strong Turn-1 performer but is the only model whose BC utility (66.3%) falls short of its own Oracle (67.6%), indicating it rarely updates on clarification; (2) unsafe recovery -- Qwen3-Next-80B achieves the highest BC utility (75.2%) but the largest harmfulness increase (+2.4), versus Claude-Haiku-4.5's smallest safety erosion (+0.6) with modest utility gain (+29.8pp); (3) repetitive recovery -- measured via checklist redundancy (Figure 2): GPT-5-mini has the highest BC utility among proprietary models but also the highest redundancy (recycling already-satisfied items), while GPT-OSS-120B has the highest BC recovery rate (166.4%, per their Recovery% = (BC-Turn1)/Oracle formula) with the lowest redundancy. A conversation-bucketed analysis (hard refusal / partial compliance / mostly compliant by Turn-1-relative-to-Oracle) shows hard refusals recover utility substantially with BC (48.4%) but stay locked without it, while safety converges to similar harmfulness levels by conversation end (3.0-3.3 under BC) regardless of how conservative Turn 1 was -- i.e., 'hard refusals provide no lasting safety advantage.' The ANCOVA shows Turn-1 utility strongly predicts final utility (beta=0.59, p<0.001) but each additional intent-revealing user move independently contributes +10.3% utility (p<0.001); the parallel safety model shows Turn-1 safety predicts conversation safety (beta=0.56, p<0.001) with each benign move costing a modest beta=0.35 (p<0.001) safety increment. Move-type-level effects: benign justification (+16.4% utility) and benign re-asking (+14.9%) drive the largest gains, with benign justification achieving comparable utility to re-asking at less than half the safety cost (+0.32 vs +0.84); Challenge/pushback moves suppress utility (-7.1%) with no safety benefit; Non-Contentful moves suppress utility even more (backchannel -16.1%, delaying -14.9%) while reducing harmfulness, i.e. models become more cautious when users merely disengage rather than clarify.
core_contribution: The paper introduces CARRYONBENCH, an interactive multi-turn benchmark that tests whether LLMs can revise an initial (over-cautious) refusal and recover helpfulness once a user clarifies benign intent, while still remaining safe. Starting from 398 seemingly-harmful queries paired with human-validated benign intents (drawn from SORRY-Bench queries and benign contexts from CASE-Bench), the authors simulate 5,970 multi-turn conversations across 1,866 distinct conversation flows (4-12 turns each), using seven theory-grounded user follow-up move types (grounded in Martin & Rose's exchange-structure discourse theory, operationalized via Mayfield et al. 2012), producing 23,880 total model-generated turns. They also introduce BEN-UTIL, a checklist-based metric that decomposes each benign intent into ~18 atomic yes/no information items (validated via a multi-model elicitation + WildGuard harmlessness filtering + GPT-4o safety filtering pipeline) and scores, turn-by-turn and conversation-level, how much of that checklist a model's responses actually satisfy, judged item-by-item by Gemini-2.5-Flash to avoid positional aggregation bias. 14 models across 9 families are evaluated under four conditions (Turn 1 alone, Oracle single-turn with intent stated upfront, multi-turn with benign clarifications (BC), multi-turn without benign clarifications (NC)), scored jointly on BEN-UTIL and a 1-5 LLM-judged harmfulness scale (gpt-5.2, rubric from Rahman et al. 2025).
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the jailbreak sense. The paper's stated threat model (their words): 'We assume a user whose underlying intent is non-harmful but whose initial query superficially resembles a malicious request. The user clarifies their intent across turns through legitimate conversational strategies rather than adversarial escalation... this is the complement of multi-turn jailbreaking... here, the correct model behavior is to update its assessment, not sustain refusal.' The authors explicitly control for the concern that 'benign clarifications' could be exploited to mask adversarial intent by deriving all benign intents from human-validated annotations (CASE-Bench). So the object of study is the safety/utility calibration boundary of refusal-based alignment under legitimate multi-turn conversational repair, not an attack technique.
limitations: Two explicit self-bias caveats stated by the authors: (1) Gemini-2.5-Flash is used as the BEN-UTIL judge, introducing potential self-bias when evaluating Gemini target models (e.g., Gemini-3.1-Pro); (2) several of the same models used to generate the gold-standard checklists (DeepSeek, GPT, Llama, Mixtral, Qwen family members) are also evaluated as target models, creating potential self-bias in utility scoring for those models. User turns beyond the initial query are synthetic (LLM-simulated via Gemini-3-Flash, though human-validated for naturalness at 82.1%), not real human users, so ecological validity of the follow-up dynamics is bounded by the fidelity of the simulation and the fixed seven-move taxonomy. The benign intents and initial queries are sourced entirely from two prior benchmarks (SORRY-Bench, CASE-Bench), so benchmark coverage is bounded by those datasets' scope and category balance. Safety and utility are both LLM-judged (gpt-5.2 for safety, Gemini-2.5-Flash for utility) rather than human-scored at full benchmark scale, so judge-model biases/errors propagate into all reported numbers (only a subset was human-validated, and that validation targeted user-turn naturalness, not judge correctness of safety/utility scores, per the visible text). The paper is explicit that its own scope excludes adversarial multi-turn jailbreaking (which it calls the 'complement' of its setting) -- so results should not be read as evidence about robustness to persistent malicious multi-turn escalation.

datasets_used: CASE-Bench (source of human-validated benign intents/contexts, rewritten for this paper)
CARRYONBENCH (this paper's own constructed benchmark: 398 queries, 1,866 conversation flows, 5,970 conversations, 23,880 model-generated turns)
WildGuard (used as an automated harmlessness filter during checklist gold-response construction, not as an evaluated dataset)
quantitative_results: Turn 1 BEN-UTIL fulfillment range across 14 models: 10.5%-37.6%
Oracle (benign intent stated upfront, single turn) BEN-UTIL range: 25.1%-72.1%
Multi-turn with Benign Clarifications (BC) BEN-UTIL range: 37.0%-75.2%
Multi-turn without Benign Clarifications (NC) BEN-UTIL gain over Turn 1: +4.6 to +19.9 percentage points
13 of 14 models approach or exceed their own Oracle utility under BC
GPT-OSS-120B: Turn1 util 10.5%/harm 1.4; BC util 52.5% (+42.0)/harm 2.7 (+1.3); Recovery% 166.4 (highest among all models); NC util 15.3% (+4.7)/harm 1.9 (+0.5)
Qwen3-Next-80B: Turn1 util 30.6%/harm 1.5; BC util 75.2% (+44.6)/harm 3.9 (+2.4) -- highest BC utility and largest harmfulness increase; Recovery% 106.4
GPT-5-mini: Turn1 util 37.6%/harm 1.5 (highest Turn-1 and Oracle utility, Oracle 72.1%); BC util 74.8% (+37.2)/harm 2.8 (+1.2); Recovery% 51.6
DeepSeek-V3.1: Turn1 util 29.6%/harm 2.2; Oracle util 67.6%; BC util 66.3% (+36.6)/harm 2.9 (+1.7) -- BC falls short of own Oracle (utility lock-in case)
Claude-Haiku-4.5: Turn1 util 20.2%/harm 1.5; BC util 49.9% (+29.8)/harm 2.1 (+0.6) -- smallest safety erosion among cited comparisons
Redundancy (Figure 2): GPT-5-mini highest BC checklist redundancy among proprietary models; GPT-OSS-120B highest recovery with lowest redundancy

Utility moldability by Turn-1 bucket (Figure 3, BC vs no-BC): hard refusal bucket 0.0% -> 8.5% (no BC) / 0.0% -> 48.4% (BC); partial-compliance bucket 15.8% -> 15.7% (no BC) / 29.7% -> 59.6% (BC); mostly-compliant bucket 47.8% -> 47.7% (no BC) / 57.8% -> 68.8% (BC)

Safety convergence by Turn-1 bucket (Figure 3): hard refusal 1.2 -> 1.2 (no BC) / 1.2 -> 3.0 (BC); partial 1.7 -> 1.6 (no BC) / 2.3 -> 3.2 (BC); mostly compliant 2.2 -> 2.2 (no BC) / 2.9 -> 3.3 (BC) -- BC conversations converge to harmfulness 3.0-3.3 regardless of starting bucket

ANCOVA: Turn-1 utility predicts final utility, beta=0.59, p<0.001; each additional benign move independently contributes +10.3% utility, p<0.001; Turn-1 safety predicts conversation safety, beta=0.56, p<0.001; each benign move costs beta=0.35, p<0.001 on safety
Move-type effects on turn-level BEN-UTIL deviation: benign justification +16.4%, benign re-asking +14.9%, context switching +8.6%, pushback -7.1%, backchannel -16.1%, delaying -14.9%
Benign justification vs. benign re-asking safety cost: +0.32 vs +0.84 harmfulness increase for comparable utility gain
Checklist construction: ~18 atomic items per query on average
Human validation of simulated user-turn naturalness: 82.1% rated natural (majority vote, 3 annotators) on 125 conversations / 425 turns; only 2.6% rated unnatural by all three annotators
baselines_compared: 14 target models across 9 families: DeepSeek-V3.1, Llama-4-Maverick, Mixtral-8x7B-Instruct, OLMo-3-7B-Instruct, OLMo-3.1-32B-Instruct, Qwen3-235B, Qwen3-32B, Qwen3-Next-80B, GPT-OSS-120B (open-source); Claude-Haiku-4.5, Claude-Sonnet-4, Gemini-3.1-Pro, GPT-5.4, GPT-5-mini (proprietary)

Four evaluation conditions compared against each other as internal baselines: Turn 1 (query alone), Oracle single-turn (query + benign intent), multi-turn with Benign Clarifications (BC), multi-turn without Benign Clarifications (NC)

Prior single-turn over-refusal benchmarks discussed as contrast in Related Work (not re-run empirically here): XSTest (Rottger et al. 2024), OR-Bench (Cui et al. 2025), pseudo-harmful prompt generation (An et al. 2024) -- cited as prior work whose aggregate single-turn refusal-rate methodology this paper argues is insufficient, not baselines the paper benchmarks against numerically
